items.question: What is the purpose of tracking train, validation, and test metrics separately?
items.answer: Train metrics show learning on fit data; validation metrics support model selection during development; test metrics provide a final held-out estimate— each split answers a different question and must not be conflated in naming or usage.

items.question: Why should you use split-prefixed metric names like val/f1?
items.answer: Prefixes prevent key collisions, enable overlaying train vs validation curves, and make cross-run comparison in experiment trackers reliable— unprefixed names hide which split a scalar came from.

items.question: How do scalar curves help detect overfitting?
items.answer: When train/loss or train/accuracy keeps improving while val/loss rises or val/f1 stalls, the curves diverge— signaling that the model fits training data better without generalizing to validation data.

items.question: Why not log test/f1 every epoch?
items.answer: Logging test metrics every epoch and using them to pick checkpoints leaks the test set into model selection— the same bias as tuning hyperparameters on test data; test should be logged once at sign-off.

items.question: What is a primary validation metric and how do you choose one?
items.answer: It is the single val/* score that ranks checkpoints— val/f1 for imbalanced classification, val/rmse for regression, val/auc for ranking— chosen before the sweep to match the business or task objective.

items.question: What is the difference between logging per step vs per epoch?
items.answer: Per-step logging captures intra-epoch instability in long deep-learning runs; per-epoch logging suits full-pass training where one validation evaluation per epoch is sufficient— both should use a documented x-axis (global_step or epoch).
